text: A striking 2013 study by Diana Tamir and Jason Mitchell found that people devote 30 to 40 percent of spontaneous thought to self-referential narrative — projecting themselves into past or future scenarios. Brain imaging showed that this self-narrative recruits the default mode network, which is suppressed during externally directed attention. The networks are competitive: the louder the inner story, the quieter the channel for present-moment perception. This neurological trade-off means that the richness of our self-concept comes at a measurable perceptual cost — we literally see less of the world when we are most absorbed in our own story.
question: What neurological trade-off did Tamir and Mitchell's research reveal about self-referential narrative?

options.text: The default mode network enhances visual processing during self-reflection
The louder the inner story, the quieter the channel for present-moment perception
Self-narrative activates the prefrontal cortex at the expense of emotional regulation
Spontaneous thought occupies more than 60 percent of waking mental activity
